figures.caption: Figure 4: LUCAS causal graph: published ground truth (a, (Daza et al., 2020)) versus the topology our pipeline discovers from the data alone (b). The two are identical up to node placement.

Table 10: SHAP Feature Attribution vs. Causal Distance to Target. Drift simulations were run on the top 5 SHAP-ranked features (K = 20 steps, δmax = −0.5). δcrit is the first δ at which Precision falls below τPrecision = 0.7 for 3 consecutive steps; robust indicates no breaking point was found within the simulation range.

Table 11: Causal Parametric Drift vs. Replacement Noise. Both approaches start from the same GCM-generated baseline. Causal drift modifies the work interfere → treatment mechanism (preserving causal structure), while replacement noise shuffles work interfere values (breaking correlations). The ∆Prec column shows the difference: negative values indicate causal drift degrades more than noise, exposing a genuine vulnerability rather than an artifact of broken dependencies. The shaded row marks the estimated breaking point (δcrit).
Figure 12: Variant C: causal graph topology obtained with αPC = 0.2 and an anti-causal domain constraint (treatment→ benefits).

Figure 13: XGBoost Robustness Curve under the Self Help scenario, using the anti-causal Variant C topology. The Breaking Point shifts to ≈ −0.40 but remains identifiable, supporting the robustness of the diagnostic.
Figure 3: Difference between the observed covariance matrix of the OSMI dataset and the covariance matrix of data generated by the unmodified Digital Twin.

Figure 6: XGBoost classifier Robustness Curve in the Self Help scenario. Metrics are computed as rolling-window averages (window size = 300). D0 for the first 3000 samples, then D1 to Dk−1 for 4000 samples, and finally DK for 3000 samples. The red dashed line marks τPrecision = 0.7.
Figure 7: Random Forest classifier Robustness Curve in the Self Help scenario. D0 for the first 3000 observations, D1 to Dk−1 for 4000 observations, then DK for 3000 observations.
Figure 11: XGBoost Robustness Curve under the Self Help scenario, using the Variant B topology (αPC = 0.2, no constraints). The red dashed line marks τPrecision = 0.7.
Figure 10: Variant B: causal graph topology obtained with αPC = 0.2 and no domain constraints.

Table 7: Aggregate Unsupervised Monitoring Summary. Max JS reports the largest Jensen– Shannon divergence across all 22 input features (excluding the target). With 22 features tested per step, exceeding τJS = 0.1 by sampling noise alone is expected—sporadic alerts appear even at baseline (k = 0–4) and do not correlate with actual drift magnitude. Two-sample KS p-values are shown for the three highest-ranked SHAP features: work interfere (wi), family history (fh), and care options (co). All pvalues remain well above 0.05, confirming no detectable distributional shift on the most predictively important features. PCA reconstruction error (5-component, threshold µ+3σ from reference data) never triggers an alert. Despite no reliable drift signal from any monitor, Precision degrades from 0.78 to below 0.70. The shaded row marks the estimated breaking point (δcrit).
Figure 8: Variant A: causal graph topology obtained with αPC = 0.1 and partial domain constraints.

Table 8: Supervised Drift Detection Under Label Delay. At 300 observations/day and 500 samples per drift step, each step spans ≈1.7 days. A 1-week delay corresponds to 4 steps, 1-month to 18 steps, and 1-year to 219 steps. The supervised detector (Precision drop > 5%) fires only after delayed labels arrive; with realistic mental-health outcome delays (months to years), it never fires within the simulation window. Our framework identifies vulnerability pre-deployment with zero label dependency. The shaded row marks the first step where the supervised detector fires with instantaneous labels.

Table 6: Univariate Drift Monitoring on the Drifted Mechanism. Jensen–Shannon divergence and two-sample Kolmogorov–Smirnov p-values are computed between reference and generated data for the two features directly involved in the causal drift: work interfere (wi, manipulated parent) and treatment (trt, target node). JS values remain far below the alert threshold (τJS = 0.1) and KS p-values stay well above 0.05 at every step, confirming that neither univariate test detects any distributional shift on the drifted mechanism despite substantial Precision degradation. The shaded row marks the estimated breaking point (δcrit).
Table 14: OSMI dataset overview, part 2.
Figure 5: Difference between the observed covariance matrix of the LUCAS dataset and the covariance matrix of data generated by the LUCAS Digital Twin.

Figure 9: XGBoost Robustness Curve under the Self Help scenario, using the Variant A topology (αPC = 0.1, partial constraints). D0 for the first 3000 observations, then D1 to Dk−1 for 4000 observations, then DK for 3000 observations. The red dashed line marks τPrecision = 0.7. Variant A is shown here as an illustrative example; the topologies and robustness curves for Variants B and C are deferred to Appendix B (Figures 10–13).
Figure 2: Causal graph topology discovered by the PC algorithm on the OSMI dataset with αPC = 0.05 and our domain constraints.
Table 13: OSMI dataset overview, part 1.

Table 9: Marginal Distribution Stability: Two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test for P (X) at each drift step. With 22 features × 21 steps = 462 tests, sporadic p < 0.05 values are expected by chance (approximately 5%). The work interfere marginal remains consistently non-significant, confirming pure concept drift (P (Y |X) changes while P (X) remains stable). The shaded row marks the estimated breaking point (δcrit).

Table 5: Extended Performance Metrics Across Drift Steps in the Self Help Scenario. All metrics are computed on 500 samples generated at each step k with drift magnitude δ. The shaded row marks the estimated breaking point (δcrit).
